title: Walks
lean_code_with_comments:
inductive Walk (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  | nil (v : Node) (hv : v ∈ G) : Walk G v v
  | cons {u w : Node} (v : Node) (a : Node × Node)
      (h : G.WalkStep u a v) (p : Walk G v w) : Walk G u w

def intoStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.into u a

def outOfStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.outOf u a

def intoEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  | none => False
  | some a => G.into v a

def outOfEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  | none => False
  | some a => G.outOf v a

def IsDirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk

def IsBidirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L ∧ p.IsBidirectedWalk

def IsColliderWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _) => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)) =>
      (a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)) ∧ p.IsColliderRest

def IsPath {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop := p.vertices.Nodup

def IsBifurcation {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i

def IsBifurcationSource {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (x : Node) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧ p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x
lean_code_without_comments:
inductive Walk (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  | nil (v : Node) (hv : v ∈ G) : Walk G v v
  | cons {u w : Node} (v : Node) (a : Node × Node)
      (h : G.WalkStep u a v) (p : Walk G v w) : Walk G u w

def intoStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.into u a

def outOfStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.outOf u a

def intoEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  | none => False
  | some a => G.into v a

def outOfEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  | none => False
  | some a => G.outOf v a

def IsDirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk

def IsBidirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L ∧ p.IsBidirectedWalk

def IsColliderWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _) => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)) =>
      (a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)) ∧ p.IsColliderRest

def IsPath {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop := p.vertices.Nodup

def IsBifurcation {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i

def IsBifurcationSource {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (x : Node) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧ p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x
lean_explanation:
The polymorphic vertex type `Node : Type*` plays the role of the LN's silent carrier $J \cup V$ — the disjoint union of contexts $J$ and value-nodes $V$ inherited from `def_3_1`. The `[DecidableEq Node]` instance is the minimal typeclass needed to talk about `Finset` membership of edges in `G.E` and `G.L`, and to decide vertex equality inside `List.Nodup` for `IsPath`. The ambient graph `G : CDMG Node` carries the directed-edge finset $G.E$, the symmetric bidirected $G.L$, and the disjoint partition $J \sqcup V$.

A walk is encoded as an `inductive` family `Walk G u v` indexed by its two endpoints, mirroring mathlib's `SimpleGraph.Walk`. The constructor `nil v hv` is the trivial walk $(v_0)$ at type `Walk G v v` — so the LN's $v = w$ precondition lives at the type level — witnessed by the membership proof `hv : v ∈ G`. The constructor `cons v a h p` prepends the alternating step "$v_0, a_0, v_1$" in front of an existing walk `p`; here `v` is the middle vertex $v_1$, `a` is the ordered-pair edge, and `h : G.WalkStep u a v` is the LN's per-edge constraint — the two-way disjunction "$a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \cup L$ or $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$" from clause (b) of LN item~i. The auxiliary `WalkStep` predicate factors this disjunction into a named helper so every downstream consumer destructures `h` once rather than re-splitting the disjunction inline.

Items (ii)–(vi) are each encoded as a `Prop`-valued predicate on `Walk`. A pair `(p : Walk G u v) (hp : p.IsDirectedWalk)` carries exactly the LN's "walk plus distinguishing sub-class constraint" data, and the forgetful "every directed walk is a walk" is the literal identity function. The trivial walk satisfies `IsDirectedWalk`, `IsBidirectedWalk`, `IsColliderWalk`, and `IsPath` vacuously, matching the LN's explicit admittance of $n = 0$ for the first three and the vacuous `Nodup` of a singleton vertex list for the last.

The four end-node classifiers (`intoStart`, `outOfStart`, `intoEnd`, `outOfEnd`) reuse `G.into` and `G.outOf` from def_3_3 rather than re-spelling the underlying set-theoretic disjunctions. The start-side ones match the `cons` head; the end-side ones reach the last edge via `p.edges.getLast?`. On the trivial walk all four return `False`, vacuously, since neither $a_0$ nor $a_{n-1}$ exists.

A handful of Mathlib idioms are worth flagging. The signature `∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop` uses strict-implicit binders that Lean's structural recursion fills in from the constructor pattern. The pattern `(p@(.cons _ _ _ _))` is an as-pattern: the recursion sees the matched subterm and can pass it on. `p.vertices[i + 1]?` is the partial list-index operator returning `Option Node`. `IsPath` reduces to `p.vertices.Nodup`; `IsBifurcation` and `IsBifurcationSource` use `List.tail` and `List.dropLast` to encode the LN's "$v_0 \notin \{v_1, \dots, v_n\}$ and $v_n \notin \{v_0, \dots, v_{n-1}\}$" non-membership clauses — strictly weaker than full `Nodup`, since interior vertices may repeat in a bifurcation.
design_choices:
**The walk type is an `inductive` family indexed by its endpoints, not a list of edges plus a coherence record.** Mirroring mathlib's `SimpleGraph.Walk`, the two-vertex index `Walk G u v` puts the trivial-walk precondition $v = w$ at the type level, and the constructor types enforce edge-endpoint coherence by construction. The data-carrying shape is essential downstream: ancestral sets, acyclicity, topological orderings, and active paths for d-/σ-separation all recurse on walks at every inductive step. A `List (Node × Node)` plus a separate coherence proof would force every consumer to destructure the pair and unpack the proof one edge at a time.

**The five walk-class predicates are `Prop`-valued on `Walk`, not separate inductive types.** The LN's items (ii)–(vi) introduce sub-classes of walks, not new walk-flavoured types: "a directed walk *is a walk* that, in addition, satisfies …" A pair `(p, hp)` carries the walk together with its sub-class constraint, and "every directed walk is a walk" becomes the literal identity. A separate `inductive DirectedWalk` would force a forgetful coercion and duplicate the structural recursion in every downstream lemma that mixes walk classes.

**The four end-node classifiers are four independent predicates, not a four-way enum or a `Bool × Bool` pair.** They are not jointly exhaustive: on the trivial walk all four return `False` (no $a_0$ exists), and a bidirected first edge $a_0 \in L$ satisfies `intoStart` but not `outOfStart`. An enum would need an ad-hoc `none` constructor; a `Bool × Bool` would compress two distinct LN clauses (the into-marker and out-of-marker) into bit positions. The classifiers reuse `G.into` and `G.outOf` from def_3_3, preserving a single source of truth for "edge into/out of a vertex".

**The $n = 1$ collider walk is restricted to a single bidirected edge.** The LN source block contains an inline note suggesting any adjacency $v \sus w$ qualifies, but the LN's symbolic pattern $v_0 \suh \cdots \hus v_n$, read literally at $n = 1$, forces arrowheads at both endpoints — i.e. a bidirected edge in $L$. The canonical tex statement resolves the inconsistency in favour of the strict symbolic reading; purely directed edges $(v, w) \in E$ or $(w, v) \in E$ are not collider walks of length 1.

**The bifurcation predicate excludes the $k = n$ with directed hinge degeneracy.** Without this exclusion, a tuple $v_n \to v_{n-1} \to \cdots \to v_0$ — a plain directed walk read right-to-left — would qualify as a "bifurcation", contradicting the visual Y-shape. The canonical tex statement adds clause (e) ("each end-node has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it"), which closes the degeneracy: a directed hinge at the last edge places its arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$, not at $v_n$. The "source" is encoded as a `Prop`-predicate `IsBifurcationSource p x` rather than an `Option Node` accessor, making the partiality structural.

**The collider and bifurcation predicates factor positional structure into auxiliary recursive helpers.** Both have a tripartite shape — first / interior / last for the collider; left arm / hinge / right arm for the bifurcation — and a single inline recursion would need boolean flags or length comparisons to track position. Factoring out `IsColliderRest`, `IsBifurcationWithSplit i`, and `IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i` keeps each predicate's recursion one-shaped, with the split index $i$ (one less than the LN's $k$) threaded as a small `ℕ` counter. `IsBifurcation` then existentially quantifies the index away, keeping the LN-faithful predicate name free of any visible split parameter.
